coredata.dc:title: Tyrosinase coupled with boron-doped carbon nanodots for fluorometric determination of dithiocarbamate fungicide ziram
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Dongwei
Liu, Donghui
Zhou, Zhiqiang
Wang, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Determination of dithiocarbamate fungicides using chromatography is not available for real-time and on-site residue analysis. Herein, a novel fluorescent assay for dithiocarbamate fungicides ziram based on the boron-doped carbon nanodots (B-CDs) and tyrosinase was established. The fluorescent B-CDs with maximum excitation and emission at 490 and 540 nm were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction. PCV can combine with the B-CDs, leading to a fluorescent quenching effect. Tyrosinase can catalyze the PCV oxidation, thus fluorescent intensity of the B-CDs recovers due to the consumption of PCV. While ziram can inhibit tyrosinase activity, resulting in less oxidation of PCV and more quenching effect of the B-CDs. Fluorescence was first employed to enzymatic ziram assay to improve sensitivity. This assay demonstrates a higher sensitivity for ziram (LOD: 0.46 μg L-1) compared to other optical assays and feasibility of determining ziram residue in environmental and food samples (Recovery: 73.9–92.6%, RSD: 2.53–6.57%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pyrocatechol violet
Boron-doped carbon nanodots
Tyrosinase
Fluorometric
Ziram

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of B-CDs. (B) TEM image of B-CDs (Zoomed). (C) Absorbance (black line), excitation (red line) and emission spectrum (blue line) of B-CDs.
XPS spectra of (A) survey scan, (B) C1s, (C) N1s and (D) B1s of the obtained B-CDs.
(A) Absorbance spectra of B-CDs, PCV and B-CDs-PCV mixture. (B) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, B-CDs-PCV, B-CDs-PCV-tyrosinase and B-CDs-PCV-tyrosinase-ziram (50 μg L-1).
(A) Fluorescence spectra of the B-CDs-PCV-tyrosinase system with various concentration of ziram. (B) Linear relation between the IEs of ziram and logarithm concentration of ziram.
Principle of the fluorometric ziram assay employing B-CDs, PCV and tyrosinase.
Detection limits of the reported optical ziram assays.
Analytical performance of the proposed assay in environmental and food samples.

head.ce:title: Tyrosinase coupled with boron-doped carbon nanodots for fluorometric determination of dithiocarbamate fungicide ziram

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, College of Science, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, College of Science, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Determination of dithiocarbamate fungicides using chromatography is not available for real-time and on-site residue analysis. Herein, a novel fluorescent assay for dithiocarbamate fungicides ziram based on the boron-doped carbon nanodots (B-CDs) and tyrosinase was established. The fluorescent B-CDs with maximum excitation and emission at 490 and 540 nm were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction. PCV can combine with the B-CDs, leading to a fluorescent quenching effect. Tyrosinase can catalyze the PCV oxidation, thus fluorescent intensity of the B-CDs recovers due to the consumption of PCV. While ziram can inhibit tyrosinase activity, resulting in less oxidation of PCV and more quenching effect of the B-CDs. Fluorescence was first employed to enzymatic ziram assay to improve sensitivity. This assay demonstrates a higher sensitivity for ziram (LOD: 0.46 μg L-1) compared to other optical assays and feasibility of determining ziram residue in environmental and food samples (Recovery: 73.9–92.6%, RSD: 2.53–6.57%).

ce:section.ce:para: Dithiocarbamate fungicides are extensively used for preventing various fungal plant pathogens in agriculture production. However, dithiocarbamate fungicides exposure was related to health risks such as thrombocytopenia [1], nervous system tumors [2], and Parkinson disease [3]. Thus, monitoring the environmental and food-residual dithiocarbamate fungicides is essential, which requires sensitive, convenient and real-time assays for dithiocarbamate fungicides. In conventional methods, dithiocarbamate fungicides are indirectly determined by gas chromatography or spectrometry analyzing carbon disulfide, the hydrolysate of dithiocarbamate fungicides [4–6]. The intrinsic sulfur-containing substances in the sample matrix might also be converted to carbon disulfide, interfering accuracy of the assays. On the other hand, the derivatization using strong acid is time-consuming, harmful and unfriendly to the environment. Therefore, sensitive assays for rapid, on-site and real-time dithiocarbamate fungicides monitoring is still required.

Optical strategies including colorimetry, fluorometry and Raman spectrometry are developed for rapid, real-time and on-site pesticide residue analysis [7]. Fluorometry is an emerging choice in optical analysis due to its high sensitivity and anti-interference to color of sample. Recently, heteroatoms-doped carbon nanodots have been widely used as fluorescent probe due to their diverse and tunable optical properties compared to pure carbon nanodots [8–10]. Doped heteroatoms can act as electron donors or acceptors, altering optical properties of carbon nanodots [11]. And when the precursors of carbon nanodots contain heteroatom groups, the synthesized heteroatoms-doped carbon nanodots might also have similar chemical properties as the precursors. Specifically, boron-doped fluorescent nanodots can be quenched by catechol substances through interaction between boronic acid group in nanodots and catechol [12,13]. We found that tyrosinase can catalyze oxidation of the catechol dyes, and established a colorimetric dithiocarbamate fungicides assay based on tyrosinase and the catechol dyes [14]. To improve sensitivity of the optical tyrosinase-based dithiocarbamate fungicides assay, herein we report a fluorescent assay based on tyrosinase, a catechol dye pyrocatechol violet (PCV) and the boron-doped carbon nanodots (B-CDs) for determining ziram, a representative of dithiocarbamate fungicides (Scheme 1 ). We revealed the quenching effect of PCV on the B-CDs. While tyrosinase can catalyze oxidation of PCV, relieving the quenching effect. Ziram inhibits tyrosinase activity and oxidation of PCV, altering fluorescent intensity of the B-CDs. Thus ziram can be determined by measuring fluorescent intensities of the B-CDs with the presence of tyrosinase and PCV. The limit of detection (LOD) of ziram by this B-CDs/PCV/tyrosinase assay is as low as ppb level (0.47 μg L-1). And the proposed assay can also determine spiked ziram in the real samples with acceptable recoveries, indicating potential application of the assay.

3-aminophenylboronic acid was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Other used chemicals were the same with our earlier work [14]. Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Absorbance spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV2600 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN electron microscope (FEI, USA) with an acceleration voltage of 200 KV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an Escalab 250Xi x-ray photoelectron microprobe (Thermo Scientific, USA). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded by a iS10 FT-IR spectroscopy (Thermo, USA).

Hydrothermal synthesis of B-CDs was conducted following the reported protocol [13]. 0.1 g of 3-aminophenylboronic acid was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, then 0.2 mL of NaOH solution (0.1 M) was added under stirring. The mixed solution was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave chamber and heated to 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large precipitates.

The experiment parameters were derived from our previous work [14]. Specifically, ziram solution (10 μL) and tyrosinase solution (25 μL, 100 U mL−1) were mixed and incubated (37 ℃, 10 min). Then, PCV solution (100 μL, 100 mg L-1) was added, and the mixed solution was diluted by the PBS solution (pH = 7.4, 100 mM, with 100 mg L-1 of EDTA) to 500 μL. After incubation (37 ℃, 15 min), 50 μL of B-CDs solution was added and the solution was diluted to 1.0 mL by the PBS solution and vortexed thoroughly. Then the fluorescent spectra were recorded.

Inhibition efficiency (IE, %) was used for ziram quantification:
(1)
I E
%
=
F -
F
ziram
/
F -
F 0
× 100
where FZiram and F0 are the fluorescent intensity of the B-CDs-PCV-tyrosinase system at 540 nm with and without of ziram. F is the fluorescent intensity of the mixture of B-CDs and PCV at 540 nm.
The proposed assay was applied to determination of spiked ziram in the real samples. The detailed method can be found in the Supplementary Materials.
Morphology of the obtained B-CDs was revealed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A and Fig. 1B, the B-CDs have a spherical shape with the size about 3 nm. And lattice fringes of B-CDs can be observed.

Chemical composition of B-CDs was studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Four peaks at 192 eV, 284 eV, 400 eV and 532 eV are found in the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2 A), which are attributed to B1s, C1s, N1s and O1s peaks, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) demonstrates four peaks, including C–B (283.9 eV), C–C/C = C (284.6 eV), C–O (285.2 eV) and C–N (285.4 eV). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) shows three peaks, which are assigned to C–N–C at 399.4 eV, N–C at 400.2 eV and N–H at 401.5 eV. In the spectrum of B1s (Fig. 2D), two peaks of B–C (191.6 eV) and B–O (192.7 eV) are deconvoluted. Composition of B-CDs was also investigated by FI-IR. In Fig. S1, the peak at 3391 cm−1 was ascribed to N–H vibrations. And the asymmetric stretching vibration of B-O was found at 1396 cm−1. The result of FT-IR indicated the presence of boron and nitrogen in B-CDs. From the fluorescent spectra of B-CDs (Fig. 3 B), optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of B-CDs are 490 nm and 540 nm, respectively. Quantum yield of B-CDs was determined and calculated to be 25.4%. Altogether, the characterization results of B-CDs are in good accordance with the previous reports, indicating a successful synthesis of B-CDs [13]. And B-CDs can be relatively stable at various pH (Fig. S2), under ultraviolet light irradiation (Fig. S3) and in high ionic strength solution (Fig. S4).Fig. 4

Fluorometric detection of ziram is based on the quenching effect of PCV towards B-CDs, tyrosinase-induced oxidation of PCV and inhibitory effect of ziram on tyrosinase. Dopamine was reported to statically quench the fluorescence of boron-doped carbon quantum dots due to the interaction between boronic acid group in the B-CDs and cis-diol structure in dopamine [10]. Thus it is reasonable to suppose that cis-diol structures in PCV also interacts with boronic acid group in B-CDs, leading to a static quenching effect. As shown in Fig. 3A, addition of PCV changes the absorbance spectrum of B-CDs, suggesting formation of new substance or complex. And we have reported that tyrosinase can catalyze oxidation of PCV, changing cis-diol structures in PCV to o-quinone structures. Thus quenching of B-CDs can be relieved because less PCV can interact with B-CDs. And the presence of ziram prevents the consumption of PCV due to its inhibitory effect on tyrosinase, leading to a decrease of the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3B).

As shown in Fig, 4A, fluorescence intensities of the B-CDs at 540 nm gradually decreases when concentration of ziram raising from 0 to 100 μg L-1. Quantification of ziram was performed by plotting the IEs of ziram with the corresponding logarithm concentration of ziram (2,5–100 μg L-1) with the linear regression equation of IE = 35.79 log [ziram] + 11.01 (R2 = 0.9962). And detection limit of the proposed fluorometric ziram assay was determined to be 0.47 μg L-1 (the 3σ rule). Compared to other reported optical ziram assays (Table 1 ), the proposed assay has a better sensitivity.

Selectivity of the B-CD-PCV-tyrosinase assay was studied to ensure the feasibility of determining ziram in complex matrices. IEs of several potential interfering substances including Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ glucose, glutamic acid, chlorpyrifos, carbofuran and atrazine (0.5 mg L-1, 50 times of ziram concentration) were determined by the B-CD-PCV-tyrosinase assay. The results of IEs (Fig. S5) reveal that these tested substances cannot interfere the B-CD-PCV-tyrosinase assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ziram in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Feasibility of real-world application of the assay was studied by the standard addition method spiking ziram in the river water, rice and apple samples. The recoveries of spiked ziram were calculated by the ratio of the detected level of ziram in the samples to the spiked level of ziram. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of the assay in the real samples were in the range of 73.9–92.6% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 2.53–6.57%, indicating the preliminary feasibility of analyzing ziram residues in environmental and food samples.

By exploring the quenching effect of PCV towards the B-CDs, we successfully established a sensitive fluorometric ziram assay using B-CDs, PCV and tyrosinase. Determination of ziram was achieved by altering the fluorescent intensity of the B-CDs through tyrosinase-induced oxidation of PCV and the inhibition effect of ziram on tyrosinase. The proposed B-CD/PCV/tyrosinase fluorometric assay has a better sensitivity for ziram (LOD: 0.47 μg L-1) compared to other reported optical ziram assays. Good selectivity toward potential interfering substances and feasibility of determining ziram in the environmental and food samples with good accuracy and precision further reveal the potential for real-time and on-site detection of ziram by this assay.

Dongwei Wang: . : Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Donghui Liu: Supervision, Resources. Zhiqiang Zhou: Supervision, Resources. Peng Wang: Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Contract Grants: 21677175)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106241.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tibial growth plate vascularization is inhibited by the dithiocarbamate pesticide thiram in chickens: potential relationship to peripheral platelet counts alteration
Environ. Sci. Pollut. Res.

Analyst
